task: I’m dealing with a new system at work that’s creating duplicate entries and a couple of scanner mistakes, and I want to avoid making the wrong call on a shipment. What’s a practical way to compare the live result against the stored record and decide whether it needs a hold or can keep moving?

turns.agent_response:
A practical way is to use a very short **compare-then-decide** check that forces a clear hold decision when traceability is questionable.

### 1) Compare live result vs. stored record
Check these in order:
- **Part / shipment ID**
- **Quantity**
- **Scanner / label match**
- **Location / pallet / box**
- **Any duplicate entry or repeated scan**

If everything matches, keep moving. If anything is off, don’t guess—move to hold criteria.

### 2) Use a simple decision rule
**Keep moving** only if:
- The live scan matches the stored record
- The label is readable and traceable
- The duplicate is clearly a harmless system duplicate, not a second physical item

**Hold** if:
- The scan and record don’t match
- There are duplicate entries and you can’t prove which one is correct
- The label/scanner result is questionable
- Traceability is in doubt

### 3) Practical floor check
Use a one-line check like:

**Match? Y/N | Duplicate? Y/N | Label/scanner okay? Y/N | Traceability clear? Y/N | Action: Move / Hold**

### 4) What to record when you hold
Capture:
- Exact mismatch type
- Which record is live vs stored
- Scanner/label issue
- Box/pallet position
- Photo if possible
- Any repeat occurrence

### 5) Rule of thumb
If the issue is only a minor system duplicate **and** you can verify the physical item and traceability, keep moving.  
If the duplicate or scanner mistake creates any doubt about identity or traceability, **hold it**.

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page checklist with a clear **hold / note-and-continue** decision line.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close, but it needs to be tighter. I’d want the check split out between **data mismatch** and **physical mismatch**. Those are not the same thing.

What I’d use:

1) **Match the physical item first**
- part number
- lot / serial
- quantity
- pallet / box ID
- label condition

2) **Then compare the records**
- live scan
- stored entry
- timestamp
- duplicate count
- any overwrite or second entry

3) **Hold it if any of these hit**
- two records claim the same unit and you can’t prove which one is valid
- scanner misread the serial or lot number
- label is smeared, damaged, or unreadable
- live count and stored count don’t reconcile
- physical pallet doesn’t match the record, even if the screen says it does

4) **Keep it moving only if**
- the part matches in hand
- the duplicate is clearly just a system duplicate
- there’s a clean paper trail showing which record is the real one
- you can explain it to audit without hand-waving

I’d also put a hard line in there: **if traceability takes extra interpretation, that’s a hold**. Don’t call it good just because the screen looks mostly right.

If you want, I can turn that into a dead-simple field checklist, like a yes/no box for the dock or inspection table.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that split is the right way to run it.

I’d use this at the bench:

### 1) Physical match
Check:
- part number
- lot / serial
- quantity
- pallet / box ID
- label condition

### 2) Record match
Check:
- live scan
- stored entry
- timestamp
- duplicate count
- overwrite / second entry

### 3) Decision
**Keep moving only if:**
- physical item matches in hand
- record matches the physical item
- duplicate is clearly just a system duplicate
- traceability is clean and explainable

**Hold if:**
- two records claim the same unit and you can’t prove which is valid
- scanner misread the lot / serial
- label is smeared, damaged, or unreadable
- live count and stored count don’t reconcile
- physical pallet doesn’t match the record
- traceability needs interpretation

### 4) Hard rule
If you have to “make it fit,” **hold it**.

If you want, I can tighten this into a one-line yes/no dock card.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s the right call. Clean split, hard line on traceability, no room for guessing. That’s what I’d want on the floor.
